en: For my presentation today, I will focus on earth-sheltered housing or underground housing as it's more commonly known.
I'll illustrate how it can meet the demands of ecological environmental planning regarding building and energy resources.
Let's first look at the advantages of earth-sheltered housing.
Perhaps the most significant merit of earth-sheltered housing is the low impact on the environment since houses built underground can hardly be seen from the surface.
Then, earth-sheltered houses are not only environmentally friendly but also surprisingly efficient regarding energy.
It is because underground housing can make great use of the alternative technologies to improve energy efficiency.
Moreover, many earth-sheltered housing projects use natural, non-toxic building materials.
Some are even more durable than standard building materials.
As a result, earth-sheltered houses have a lifespan of 500 years or more.

This type of building technique means less exposure to the outside elements like sunshine, storm, snow etc., so it can give the houses actual protection and keep the maintenance of the houses to the minimum.
Earth-sheltered houses also give people a quiet environment.
As we know, noise is a severe problem in modern society, and many people consider moving home because of this.
In that case, earth-sheltered houses might be a good choice because living underground means those people will no longer suffer from the disturbances caused by the traffic on the road.
And lastly, earth-sheltered houses also bring significant health advantages to people.
Because of the sophistication of the ventilation systems installed in earth-sheltered houses, it provides a comfortable living environment for people who have asthma and other chest diseases.
Then let's move on to earth-sheltered housing in the UK.
Despite all the apparent advantages we mentioned above, earth-sheltered housing still isn't a popular choice for most British families, as the statistics show.
The British Earth-sheltering Association tries to persuade people to try this type of accommodation, saying that it's easier to get planning permission for an earth-sheltered house.
In contrast, for a conventional home, there is always too much red tape.
A lot of evidence shows that the building development offices are more sympathetic to earth-sheltered projects, especially in the conservation areas.
On the sites where conventional houses aren't usually allowed to build, earth-sheltered housing might be a feasible solution.
And there is even a possibility that one day, earth-sheltered houses can take the place of mobile homes along the coastlines in the UK.
In the last part, I'd like to talk about two different projects in the UK: Mole Manor and Hockerton.
When it was first built, Mole Manor attracted wide publicity around the world.
A newspaper poll conducted by the Earth-sheltering Association showed that Mole Manor was voted as one of the twelve most desirable places to live in the world.
Similarly, Hockerton aroused considerable interest from the international media as well, winning several awards for its innovative environmental design.
The running cost for Mole Manor is much lower than that of conventional houses.
It is because Mole Manor is installed with an effective insulation system, significantly reducing its heating bill.
While at Hockerton, all the basic needs of families living there are satisfied by energies in this area.
For example, wind power, solar energy and rain are used to generate enough warmth, water for washing and drinking, and electricity for lighting and cooking.
As a result, people there can live a lifestyle in harmony with the environment.
It is also proved that at Hockerton, earth-sheltered houses can be built on a modest budget because it gives priority to cost when designing the houses.
But it is not the case with Mole Manor, which pays more attention to style than cost.
Compared with the homely cottage style at Hockerton, the houses at Mole Manor are expensive, beautiful and comfortable, and it is said that these houses are worth more than 800,000 pounds today.
In conclusion, Mole Manor and Hockerton Projects show that earth-sheltered housing has numerous advantages.
They can not only provide comfortable and stylish houses to people but are also beneficial to the environment.
And with the help of new energy-saving technology, it can meet all the criteria for ecological and environmental planning.
